text: The surgeon begins the sacral dissection, preparing the area for the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon makes a small incision in the peritoneum overlying the sacrum, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon ensures that the surgical site is distal to the bifurcation of the common iliac arteries during the rectopexy procedure.
The surgeon uses a PK device to apply pressure and visually identify the location of the sacral promontory.
The surgeon carefully navigates through the adipose tissue, searching for a small window between the fatty lobules to proceed with the dissection.
The surgical field reveals finger-like projections of presacral fat, rather than a continuous sheet, as the surgeon navigates the area.
The surgeon identifies small openings between the finger-like projections of presacral fat, using these windows to approach the anterior ligament.
The anterior longitudinal ligament is visible, located near the promontory, with surrounding finger-like projections of presacral fat.
